sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah, I came to the front and he said, come sit next to me.
I take you somewhere.
And he went on the road which is going, also, to the crematoria, and also to the camp C.
But because he frightened me all the time, what did you do?
I said, I did everything to the best of the good of the people and good of the camp.
But what did you do wrong?
[GERMAN].
That means-- you can't translate to English.
How-- what did you do?
Just say it in-- in--
Spiteful or something wrong.
So I-- and I just repeated, always, I did, always, the best.
And meantime, we arrived to the gate of the C.
So when I saw him turn to the C, so I knew that he's not taking me to the gas chamber.
So he said now, if you did everything for the best of the camp and best of the people, so here you will be again, lageralteste.
So this time the people wants a weaving.
Weaving?
Weaving.
Weaving.
People weaving, [INAUDIBLE], they call.
So I don't know, they weaved something for-- I think for the ships over.
I didn't-- I don't even know.
Block-- no, that was the same camp.
Same camp.
That was October.
And that lasted till January the 15th, when they send us on the death march.
I became the lageralteste again, from this camp of those weaving people, weaving-- weavers.
There was not in a very bad condition.
Because they had been like they-- they did that for the Germany.
They needed them.
That's the same like they would send them to Germany.
But they did it here.
It's a funny thing.
More chutzpah you are, more they respect you.
Because they had that opinion of Jews that they are frightened and scared.
And they are not daring.
And I was daring.
And I said to everything, and-- and he-- we--
Beg pardon?
I don't know.
I don't know.
I don't know if-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look, after all, I was not that bad looking girl.
I was blonde and slim and tall, and had a big mouth.
But I don't know if what made it.
Never.
Never.
The same thing, yeah.
Beg pardon?
And then what happened?
Then came the news that the-- the Russians are coming close.
So now was different news.
Either that they will detonate the whole camp with people in it.
Or they will send them on a death march.
So suddenly came the news that they are sending all the camp on the death march.
Not only this camp, but all camps under the death march.
Also men.
And so when that happened, that the girls, the stronger girls, they broke in the kitchen.
And they took out some salami, some margarine, some sugar, some bread.
And they had-- I don't know where they had the rucksacks.
Maybe there in the certain room.
But they fill that and they went on a death march.
It was terrible there.
That death march, first of all, it was winter.
It was terrible cold.
And they marched us quickly.
Because they was running away from the Russians.
So who was a little bit weaker, or who was behind?
Even just made himself the shoelaces, they shoot him on the spot.
Did you have food with you?
Yes.
Because one of the girls in our group, she was working in the kitchen.
So she knew where everything was.
So she brought out salami and margarine and sugar.
And they divided.
And in the rucksack, they carried it.
And when there was a possibility, then we gave everybody a piece of bread, and so.
But it was-- it was terrible.
Really terrible.
I tried, with the group of the girls who was around me, to push them to the front.
Because one of the girls was limping.
So I thought when they will see her limping, they will shoot her.
So we went to the front.
And a interesting thing happened.
That we crossed with the men.
You know, in one crossing, we crossed the men.
And meantime, the girls knew that my husband, like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
--future-- future idea for me.
So they asked the SS woman if that she has a husband, could she talk to him five minutes?
So one said no, I can't talk to my husband.
And she went around to all SS woman.
One SS woman said OK, for five minutes, here next to the rock.
So he came to the rock.
And he said, if he survive remember my-- my number.
And I said, yeah, I was good sport to everybody.
So I said, I can't remember numbers.
Because I didn't believe-- were are we?
Free?
Where are we?
Phew.
So he said, but I tell you how to remember my number.
Because my number is in your number.
I said, how?
Then he said, now look, you have 2318.
And when you count my numbers, 3 times 6 and 5 is 23.
And then 3 times 6 is 18.
So you say it three times, and then you will never forget.
I said, but how?
Imagine that after so much long, we thought in case we will be liberated, we still will be numbers.
It's unbelievable.
Normal-thinking people was thinking, remember my number.
Anyway, and then-- then we went and we came to Ravensbruck.
And--
One week.
Something like that.
What happened?
One night, we rested.
In a-- a-- at-- at the peasant place, where he had the straw and all this thing.
You know, like, hay.
And we rested there.
And so we had a little food.
So we ate food.
And--
Beg pardon?
They didn't care about anybody.
They just marched and slow, slow, slows, that's what they want.
Because they wanted to be further as possible from the Russian coming.
Yeah.
--that the Russians would take them over.
Yeah.
When we were in the Ravensbruck.
So they made there a big tent which was just in the-- on the earth, which is mud, in the mud.
And nothing-- nothing in it, cold, freezing, no food, nothing.
I was lucky because the French woman who, one stage, was in Birkenau, and I was there, the blockalteste.
They was looking-- they thought they heard that Birkenau came.
So they was looking for me.
I was really sick already.
So they took me to their barrack.
And there was doctors and nurses.
And so they bathed me and nursed me and fed me and dress me.
And I had to rest there all day, just for the roll call, I had to go then.
And so that's made me survive.
After a few days we march-- they marched us further on.
Beg pardon?
I don't know how many, a few days.
And then we marched further on.
And we went to Malchow, a small camp there.
And a funny thing, that on the way one SS man-- but he wasn't the SS man.
He was a Wehrmacht.
He asked me what are you, from profession?
I said, what do you need my profession here?
Just tell me, what is your profession?
I said, kindergarten teacher.
You, I am looking for.
Why?
Because there are 40 young girls.
So when we are coming to the camp, I will tell the lagerfuhrerin in charge that you will be in charge of the 40 girls.
So the 40 girls was part of-- of the 800 I saved.
Mengele, in Birkenau, couldn't stand the young girls.
So he said, all the 800 girls to put in barrack number 3, who was empty.
Because part of them went to the transport, to Germany.
And some, to the gas chambers.
And it became empty.
So he put the 800 girls there.
And in the morning, early hours, he wanted them to send to the gas chambers.
Just like that.
He put a SS guard, before, to watch it.
And I was very upset.
And I said, this is incredible.
This was my apple of my eye, how you say, of my eye.
And we have to do something.
So what do you have to do?
We have to look for a drink.
So I went to the last barrack, from one to another.
And in the last barrack I found drink.
Because one German, he was-- --many years there.
Because he-- I don't know if he was a robber or something.
But otherwise, he was a nice man.
And he was working there with a commando.
But he had a little rest in this barrack.
And there was a young girl, as a blockalteste.
She was 16 years old.
And she was in charge.
So he was very nice to her.
Because he-- he behaved, to her, like a father.
And he left, always, food for her, and a drink for next day.
So I said, listen.
I'll take the drink.
And you tell to him I took it.
So I-- we brought a drink.
And I spoke with the-- and report [INAUDIBLE].
Because it was-- nobody was in the camp there.
It was a Sunday afternoon.
So no SS man was in.
Just in the front.
So I said, go and start talk to him.
So he started talk to him.
And it worked out that he has to go to-- to the war, to Russia.
So I said, that's a good point.
Now I am going to-- to-- with him.
And I take him a little drink.
And I said to him, I'm so sorry that you have to go to the camp-- to the front, Russia.
But here, have a little drink and be merry.
You don't know what will happen.
Forget about it.
Maybe everything will be all right.
So he said, thank you.
Thank you, that's good.
And then [?
Gerda ?] went.
And then I went.
And then I gave him the bottle.
He drank the bottle.
And he fell asleep and fell over.
I said, [?
Gerda, ?] now is our chance.
We went to the-- to the other end of the barrack.
I knocked on the door.
They opened the door.
And I put 100 girls down and walked with me.
I didn't say anything.
I came to a barrack.
And I said to the blockalteste there, count 100 girls more to your-- to your number.
And that's how I divided the 800 girls to the barrack.
To a different-- yeah.
Because I didn't want to overload one.
Beg pardon?
Yeah.
I was the lageralteste.
And then we went to sleep.
And I don't know, to this day, how that nobody was inquired what happened.
And not either this man, what happened with him.
We're being quiet.
They're being quiet.
And and so when we march-- so who knows where-- where they came.
Because the camp was dissolved.
So May.
But 40 was keeping together.
And so it's things like that you have--
Beg pardon?
The 40 didn't help me.
The 40, when we came to the camp, Malchow, saw the SS men tell-- told the woman commander that I should be the blockalteste, to those 40 women.
Eh, OK.
And so I was.
But not for long.
Because one woman-- [LAUGHS] --this one who was once in a time in the barrack, where the high-society barrack was.
She was a runner.
She was a Jewish girl.
But from punishment, they sent her to Malchow.
Because she did something, politics.
And then she saw me.
Magda, hallelujah.
How are you?
So and she was in good friendship with the commander.
And said, let's the other-- the 40 women-- have somebody else.
But you take, rather, the 1,000 people.
Because for them, it's easier to take charge.
So I-- there was a room, a big room there.
It used to be a-- um, I don't know what it was.
But 2,000 people had rooms in it.
And divided here was like, a number 10 and that was number 11.
And I was responsible for number 10.
So again, the food, the this, the that.
And then a woman came to-- a different lagerkommandant came to the camp.
A woman who was a sadist-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Hmm?
Eh, that was-- wait, wait a minute. '45, March.
Anyway, she was running around the camp with the whip and whipping girls because she was in my [INAUDIBLE], doing the same thing.
She was a terrible woman.
But she had respect for me, again.
Because one day, the next camp dismantled.
And the staff wanted my position.
And they, being very friendly with that woman-- that she was a terrible woman.
And she came to our camp.
And she put me down.
And put the other girls in.
All my girls out.
She put hers in.
But that man, that German, who-- whose drink I was taking, he was so long in that camp.
So he was with SS, such a friendship.
And he told them like to a friend.
And he was sending me the message with this-- for-- with that girl, that Magda, in three days you will be back in charge.
And he arranged that.
And in three days, I was back in charge.
Those girls was out.
She was out.
And my commander, the Grese, was back.
And I didn't back her when she threw me out.
I didn't back here.
Oh, why do you-- oh, you-- and she remembered there.
When she came to Malchow, she called me.
She said, you know, I have a respect for you.
When I threw you out from that position, after all you've been in charge.
And it's a good feeling, even, that you have the might over so many people, and helping them.
You didn't ask me why or what?
You just took it and lived further on.
And so I thought, what a woman she is.
And what they was looking for evidence.
A woman who puts the commandos to work.
So there was other candidates.
And she choose me.
And she told me why she choose me.
Because I didn't back her for the position.
But I said to myself, you are a terrible girl, woman, even if you put me in position.
Who cares?
I was very well there too.
But I will look after it that you shouldn't run like that around the camp.
So I had a-- a friend, who lives here in Melbourne, [INAUDIBLE].
She was a portrait painter.
Not with-- only with charcoal.
So I said to her, one day, you know, I have a girl who could make a very good portraits.
You mind to have a portrait made?
She said, is she good?
I said, yes.
So bring her in.
And to that girl, [INAUDIBLE] says-- I said, don't rush.
Make it slow.
Because till you are doing the painting, she's not running with the whip around.
But that finished too.
And I said, now she will go to run around.
So I said, come with another proposition.
You know, in camp they are always reading from palms, for telling you the future.
So I said-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
Danz ?] was her name.
I said to the commandant, [?
Danz, ?] I have a woman who will tell you your future.
And everybody wants to know what it is.
She said, is she good?
I said, yes.
She told me my future too.
All right, bring her in.
So I prepared her.
Add What she told me, in the days, about her life, and about her brother who has in the front, and how her worries about him.
I told her all that.
So then this woman was telling her that, little of that, and that.
A little bit, you know, this way, that way.
But she said, I see there a brother who is very far away, in danger.
But he is even more afraid of your future.
So next day she said, imagine, she knew that I have a brother.
And-- and he's worrying about me.
Why you think he's worrying about me?
Do you think he's worrying because the war is going to end and I have a bad reputation?
That I am beating the people with the whip?
I said, who knows?
It could be.
So what do you think?
I shouldn't beat the people?
You like your brother?
Very much.
He's the only thing that I have in life.
No, that if you want to please him.
No, by what will happen?
Don't beat the people.
You can do any other things but that.
From that moment, she didn't beat them.
[LAUGHS] That's what you had to do.
So on the day of March-- we'll go back to the death marching.
Hmm?
The death march.
Now it-- it was, again, that the Russian are coming close.
So they marched us again.
From-- no.
From Malchow, further on, inside Germany.
But I didn't march too long.
Because a Russian woman who, once in a while, they had been in my barrack.
And they was Partisans.
They looked for me.
And they found me.
Because they looked.
Magda, Magda, Magda.
So he said, Magda, we came to pick you up.
Because the Russians are very close.
In a day or two, they will be here.
But if somebody points a finger of you, they not only will rape you.
But they will beat-- tear you in pieces.
Because you, they consider you a German helper.
And they are very bad with them.
Yeah.
But they consider that I help the Germans.
But we know that you helped the people.
So we are going to hide you in the woods.
Because there are-- we already know there is a mill.
We saw it.
There is plenty food.
We are going to settle there.
And close, while we are going to hide you.
So they didn't want to take all.
They took three girls.
They put us between the woods.
They made branches.
So they made like, a little house.
And you will stay there till we explain to the Russians, when they come, who you are.
And really, the Russian arrived the same day.
So they was telling them.
First they were settling down.
And then they was telling them about us.
And how much we helped them, and so-- and so where are they?
So we-- we hid them.
Because we been afraid.
If you are not doing anything to them, you bring them out.
Why should we, if they-- Keep going.
If they helped you so much?
So we want them.
So they came.
And they usually-- they brought us, always, food and drink.
Because we couldn't go out of it.
And then they--
Only three of us, was.
Three of us, the one who-- who helped me when I had that typhus--
Who organized it, all the things.
Ruzjenca.
And the other was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --it was her cousin.
But she was the one who was working in the kitchen.
And he supplies us with all the food, what we survived, in.
And so they came to pick us up.
And they put us to the half table.
And they didn't let us to do anything.
Oh, no-- no cooking, no washing.
And we had to sit with them and talk.
But they weren't there too long.
They filled in everything, what there was in the cellar.
And they went with the Russian girls to quiz them.
And he said, now you are liberated.
You walk towards the Czech people.
So--
Hmm?
The date?
It must be 6th of May.
And then--
